data.title: UnitedHealth: Recovery Is Progressing, But Upside May Be Limited Near Term
data.description:
The key question going into the Q1 2026 print was whether UnitedHealth’s margin recovery story (especially around the Medical Care Ratio and Optum Health) remains intact. After the call, my impression was that the Q1 2026 results were supportive. EPS came in ahead of expectations, and full-year guidance was raised; so, these are early indications that the company is returning to the historical “beat and raise” pattern.

I still think the margin recovery story is intact but remains in the early innings. Getting back to historical profitability will take time, and there is still execution risk. But management seems to be making the right moves.

Key Takeaways

Revenue Growth—Low, But Not The Point

Revenue growth came in at just ~2%, a clear slowdown versus historical levels. However, I don’t view this to be the key driver of the investment case. UnitedHealth is no longer a growth story; it is a margin recovery story.

Medical Care Ratio—The Key Signal Was Positive

The medical care ratio (MCR) improved to 83.9% from 84.8% in Q1 2025. That’s clearly positive. The improvement was driven by better medical cost management and favorable reserve development, among others. On the latter, I’m not fully convinced that this should be viewed as a durable improvement. However, I don’t think this is too much of a concern; the ratio is improving, which is what matters for now.

Seasonality also plays a role here. UnitedHealth’s insurance business is typically more profitable in the first half of the year. MCR will likely move higher over the year, which is not a sign of deterioration.

Operating Cost Ratio—Higher, But For The Right Reasons

The operating cost ratio increased. This looks like a bad development. However, costs are going up because the company is investing in processes, technology (like AI), and infrastructure to boost efficiency.

I’m not too concerned about this at this stage. It looks more like part of the ongoing effort to get margins back to normal.

Optum Health Remains The Weak Spot

My interpretation of Optum is that it remains the most challenged part of the business. Especially Optum Health.

Operating margin at Optum declined to 5.1% (vs. 6.1% in Q1 2025), which shows that the segment continues to have issues. Management mentioned that all three divisions (Health, Insight, and Rx) performed ahead of their expectations, which provides some comfort. But visibility on the recovery (especially in Optum Health) remains limited.

In my opinion, this continues to be the key area to watch. The overall earnings profile of UnitedHealth will likely remain in a transition phase as long as margins in Optum Health don’t stabilize.

What This Means For The Thesis

Overall, this quarter supports the margin recovery story. The company is moving in the right direction, but getting back to previous profitability will take some time.

The positive is that there were no major surprises. Utilization remains elevated but within (management’s) expectations; pricing improved, but medical cost trends still appear to run above pricing. Management continues to guide towards increasing margins into 2027. In that sense, this was a clean quarter.

My interpretation is that the weaker financial performance is less a sign of weaker earnings power and more a reflection of timing and execution issues, which tend to come with a lag between rising costs and pricing adjustments.

That distinction matters. Historically, periods of elevated medical costs have been followed by pricing catch-up—but with a delay. I see the current environment as part of that cycle and not a break in the model.

However, the recovery in earnings still looks a bit uneven. This is mainly due to ongoing issues in Optum Health.

Optum Health is clearly the main area to watch. Execution has been weak, but I do not see evidence that the model itself is broken. If anything, this looks more like a period where growth ran ahead of operational discipline and is now being corrected.

To me, the current volatility in earnings and margin pressure looks temporary. As execution improves and cost trends stabilize, margins and earnings should become more predictable again.

Valuation

At around ~19x NTM earnings, UnitedHealth does not appear very cheap. The stock continues to trade at the upper end of historical ranges. This is despite the fact that margins have not yet fully normalized.

To account for potential lag in forward estimates, I also look at the LTM multiple, which currently is closer to ~26–27x. This, I view as evidence that the market is already pricing in a meaningful recovery in profitability.

At current levels, the stock does not appear cheap and already reflects improving fundamentals. In my view, this limits near-term upside and shifts the focus to execution. Returns will therefore likely be driven more by earnings delivery than by multiple expansion. So, the investment case relies less on valuation and more on the company delivering on its margin recovery.

Risks

The key risk remains execution in Optum Health, where margin recovery is still uncertain and a slower-than-expected turnaround would reduce overall earnings quality.

Also, medical cost trends remain elevated, and if utilization and cost trends continue to outpace pricing, the margin recovery could stall.

Finally, valuation leaves limited room for error, as much of the recovery already appears in the current multiple.

Conclusion

In my view, UnitedHealth remains a high-quality compounder that is currently going through a transition phase. The return to a “beat and raise” pattern (even if still early) is a positive signal. Although the margin recovery is not yet complete, the direction of travel is clearly improving.

However, valuation is not particularly compelling, as much of the improvement seems to be already reflected in the current multiple. This likely limits near-term upside. In my view, future returns will therefore depend less on multiple expansion and more on the company delivering on its margin recovery through better execution and pricing.

For long-term investors, I still see the current phase as an opportunity to build exposure to a strong business, even if the path will take time. I maintain a Buy rating.

data.title: Q1 Snapshot Of Amerant Bancorp Is Still Not A Compelling Picture
data.description:
Amerant Bancorp Inc. (AMTB) reported its Q1 2026 earnings after the close of the market on April 23. The company’s profit of $0.44 per share topped expectations by $0.01. Revenue of $97.7 million missed previous estimates by $3.1 million. Total gross loans and deposits for the bank holding company increased slightly in the first three months of the year, but a few other key performance metrics took a sizeable step back.

I have written about Amerant Bancorp on two other occasions, and each time, I have rated its stock as a Hold. Admittedly, AMTB has performed better than I expected, outpacing the S&P 500 (SP500) by roughly 20% since my first analysis was published on June 3. The market has reacted positively to a number of steps the company has taken in the last 12 months, including cutting back on its mortgage operations in 2025 and the announcement of a new three-year strategic plan at the start of 2026.

When I wrote a follow-up article on the company in August of last year, I noted that the company did appear to be moving in the right direction. Unfortunately, a look at the complete results from Q1 2026 does not yet reveal a compelling picture. I am content to stay on the sidelines for AMTB until the company can show improvement in several of the areas that I will discuss in this analysis.

Company Overview

Amerant Bancorp operates as the holding company for Amerant Bank and Amerant Investments. Its banking operations have been in place for over 45 years. The geographic footprint for Amerant Bank is confined to Central and Southeast Florida, with two branches in Tampa and 21 additional branches along the state’s South Atlantic coast. It is the seventh-largest commercial bank in Florida, with total assets of $9.9 billion as of March 31. Just six months ago, the company reported $10.4 billion in assets, with a shrinking loan portfolio accounting for a sizeable portion of that decline.

Gross loans held for investment at Amerant Bank were valued at $6.6 billion at the end of Q1 2026. At the end of Q3 2025, that total was $6.9 billion. One year ago, the gross loan amount was equal to almost $7.2 million. The progression of that decline can be seen in the graph below. Not only has the portfolio decreased in size, but the yield on the total loans has also dropped, moving from 6.84% in Q1 2025 to 6.38% in Q1 2026. The company has made a conscious effort to prioritize asset quality in recent quarters, which has led to the removal of some of the higher-risk exposures from the bank.

About 72.2% of the loan mix for AMTB is connected to some kind of real estate. Commercial real estate loans from the bank total $2.3 billion, with about two-thirds of that total being non-owner-occupied CRE. Almost $1.7 billion is committed to residential mortgages. An additional $1.5 billion is allocated to commercial loans. From this group, owner-occupied CRE has seen the greatest relative decline in loan allocations over the last year, with a drop of 16.9%.

Deposits held by the bank have declined over the last year as well, but they did see a slight uptick in the first quarter of this year. The total in Q1 of $7.9 billion was about $200 million less than Q1 2025 but $150 million more than Q4 2025. Non-interest-bearing deposits comprise 18.5% of Amerant’s most recent total.

AMTB pays a quarterly dividend of $0.09, an amount that has not changed since Q1 2022. The current dividend yield for the stock is 1.5%, well below its regional banking peers. The company still receives low scores from Seeking Alpha’s Quant rating for dividends, although I believe the safety score may be skewed by other, less relevant factors. AMTB earned $1.25 in GAAP earnings in FY 2025 and $1.73 in adjusted earnings, which gave the company enough cushion to safely pay its $0.36 annual distribution in 2025.

Analysis of Amerant Bancorp

The overall results from Amerant Bancorp in Q1 2026 were mixed. In many areas, they did look noticeably better than the previous quarter. However, that three-month period included a number of non-recurring expenses related to operational restructuring.

Here are some key highlights from Q1 compared to Q4 from last year, none of which are positive for the company.

  • Net interest income declined $9.9 million to $80.3 million.
  • Non-interest income declined $4.6 million to $17.4 million.
  • Net interest margin declined 23 basis points to 3.55%.
  • Tangible book value for the company declined $0.18 to $22.38.

The decrease in net interest income can be attributed, in part, to a greater cost of domestic deposits and the decline in loan yield mentioned earlier. The decrease in non-interest income can be partially explained by a $3.3 million gain on the sale (and leaseback) of two bank branches that took place in Q4. If there is one positive to report here, it would be the company’s improvement in $5.5 million of non-interest income from its brokerage and advisory fees, which grew 3.8% from Q4 and 17% from Q1 2025.

The company made an effort to take advantage of its lower tangible book value with share repurchases in Q1. During the first three months of this year, Amerant bought back almost 860,000 common shares at a weighted price of $21.77 per share. This was only 0.97x of AMTB’s tangible book value at the time of the purchases and 0.95x of its book value. As I write this analysis today, the stock trades at a price-to-TBV ratio of 1.06, which certainly makes the investment thesis look more interesting considering this is well below the average for U.S. banks, which stood at 1.37 at the end of March.

Unfortunately for AMTB shareholders, the current discount given to the valuation of the stock seems to be justified considering the operational metrics for the bank that are still well below average. Return on assets at the end of Q1 was just 0.73%. Return on equity was 7.63%. Both numbers were considerably higher than their comparison from Q4 because of the charges taken during that period, but there is still more work to be done here.

Asset quality continues to be a struggle for the bank as well, even after the adjustments made last year. Amerant’s ratio of nonperforming assets equaled 1.93% in Q1 2026, two basis points higher than in Q4. Nonperforming loans stood at 2.61% in Q1, five basis points higher than the last three-month period. The company’s allowance for credit losses only moved up one basis point to 1.21%, leaving a challenging 45% ratio between what the company has allowed for losses and its total non-performing loans. Any purchase of AMTB would have to be considered high-risk until these non-performing ratios can be decreased significantly.

Additional Risks to Consider

Amerant Bancorp is heavily dependent on the economy in Florida, especially the southeast corner of the state. Almost 90% of the bank's loans are connected to South Florida, and another 5% originated in Tampa. Challenges for the business sector in the Miami-Dade County area could put pressure on the loans outstanding from the bank. AMTB has a high share of non-owner-occupied commercial real estate loans that depend on businesses making a profit to pay their rents. Even owner-occupied CRE could face headwinds from higher interest rates, rising inflation, and decreases in consumer confidence. The state of Florida is also facing additional challenges caused by a multi-year decline in population growth that has been caused by a decline in the flow of both domestic migration and international inflows.

The housing market in Florida, including South Florida, has come under pressure over the last year as inventory levels for homes, especially condominiums, remain elevated. A recent report from TD Economics showed a 5.2-month supply of homes on the market and a 9.7-month supply of condos. While mortgage rates dipped at the start of this year, they have climbed back up well above 6%, adding pressure on home affordability. Residents of Florida, especially along the coastal areas, are also under constant threat of rising insurance rates caused by natural disasters.

The Bottom Line

Amerant earned $1.73 per share in FY 2025 and is expected to bring in $2.08 in FY 2026. The number for this year has been revised down three times in the last 90 days, showing some added pessimism for the company’s outlook. Assuming the company is able to hit its earnings target for this year, that would reflect a 20.2% increase from 2025, but given the company’s recent history and the results from Q1 of this year, that does not appear to be an easy assumption.

The stock’s low valuation relative to its TBV does present a great deal of opportunity here if the company can truly get things back on the right track. AMTB could also be an interesting buyout target for a larger bank that was looking for penetration into South Florida. Those possibilities are just not enough for me to raise my rating from its current Hold.

data.title: Medpace Holdings: Downgrade To 'Buy' As Revenue Growth Remains, But With Caution Ahead
data.description:
The last time I wrote about Medpace Holdings, Inc. (MEDP), it was with a Seeking Alpha article entitled "Medpace: Full CRO Model Builds Net Revenue Growth, Maintain At Strong Buy." With respect to this article, I mentioned that it continued to deliver robust revenue growth of $23.7% year-over-year (YoY) in Q3 of 2025. Things were good for the company then, and I had given this stock a "Strong Buy" rating. However, today I'm going to downgrade the stock to a "Buy" rating. There are several reasons why I believe I should do so. The company is still achieving revenue growth for sure, and it has guided higher for 2026 as well, for starters. Thus, I believe it is expected to do well based on the guidance it has given.

However, there are some issues in place, which could become major issues. Thus, I'm placing a "Buy" rating but with some cautions ahead. The stock traded lower after earnings, despite beating on both the top and bottom lines of expectations. What may have also driven the stock to trade lower may have been the fact that the president of the company, Jesse Geiger, is set to retire on May 31, 2026. There are some key items that should be watched closely, which might bring about caution ahead. For starters, the Net Book-to-Bill incrementally dropped a bit in Q1 of 2026 from Q1 of 2025. This was because of high cancellations that occurred.

The second item would be in terms of the backlog conversion rate. It definitely did increase in Q1 of 2026 compared to Q1 of 2025, but it will be important to see how this plays out going forward. The thing of importance would be the highest number of backlog cancellations observed in over a 1-year period. If such cancellations continue, then I would be inclined to change the rating of this stock. For now, I think it is okay because every other metric I'm about to go over below has been increasing. Especially "Net New Business Awards" are increasing, along with that of revenues for Q1 of 2026. Based on the ability to continue to generate revenue growth in Q1 of 2026 YoY, plus higher guidance, I believe that this stock should remain with a "Buy" rating.

Medpace CRO Model Continues To Generate Revenue Growth In Q1 Of 2026

Medpace is a contract research organization (CRO) that has done well to build several divisions that help companies to reach their clinical goals. With that said, it has several divisions that each have their own classification. The service of being a full CRO service is to offer the ability to help establish drug candidates through clinical trials using a variety of aspects, such as:

  • Therapeutics area - trial design and implementation for a wide range of indications ranging from rheumatology, oncology, hematology, ophthalmology, nephrology, rare diseases, and more.
  • Labs - Central lab and bioanalytical lab.
  • Core Labs - Imaging core lab.
  • Phase I Unit - Established in 2008 to bridge the gap between early-stage clinical trials and late-stage trials, improve cost structure, and help with clinical outcomes.

The good thing about this company is that its revenues in Q1 of 2026 ended up being positive. The reason why I state this is because its revenue growth during this recently reported Q1 of 2026 was $706.6 million, which ended up being an increase of 26.5% compared to Q1 of 2025. This number is important for another reason. With this particular revenue achieved, this resulted in a backlog conversion rate for Q1 2026 of 23.3% (Medpace Q1 of 2026 Financial Results Presentation, slide #4). This was an increase compared to the same quarter in the prior year and in line with Q3 & Q4 of 2025.

A good thing is that "Net New Business Awards" reached $618.4 million in Q1 of 2026, which was a 23.7% increase from the year prior of $500 million. This is definitely a good thing, but more was needed here. That's because this reported Net New Business Awards is what resulted in the Net Book-To-Bill ratio ending up as 0.88x for the reported quarter. Not only did this metric begin to significantly fall off in Q3 of 2025, where the ratio of this was at 1.20x, but it is also slightly below the ratio of 0.90x in Q1 of 2025. A big reason why this metric fell off is because of high cancellations, and this is what I mentioned in the beginning of this article. Another cause for concern and reason for the downgrade from a "Strong Buy" to "Buy" rating besides high cancellations would be direct costs. This number increased substantially and should also be watched closely. In Q1 of 2026, total direct costs ended up being $510.3 million, compared to the year prior, with only $380.2 million. This means that costs increased by 34%. The point here is that revenue growth was achieved, and as I will mention below, despite some cancellation headwinds to consider, it has guided higher for its full-year 2026 earnings. Plus, the company indicated that it intends to roll out new pipeline initiatives and improve upon its win rate in the coming quarters.

Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC filing, Medpace Holdings had cash and cash equivalents of $652.7 million as of March 31, 2026. It is definitely doing well as a business because it saw its operating cash flow increase for the quarter. In Q1 of 2026, operating cash flow (OCF) was $151.8 million (Medpace Q1 of 2026 Financial Results Presentation, slide #10).

The reason for maintaining a "Buy" rating and not a "Hold" rating for this stock is because there was a positive outcome to earnings. Consider that it was not only able to beat the top line but also the bottom line of its earnings. Revenues coming in at $706.6 million beat expectations by $9 million, and then GAAP EPS of $4.28 beat the consensus estimates by $0.40. The strong execution in revenue was because of certain target areas as part of its pipeline as a CRO. This would be in terms of the categories of Metabolic, Oncology, Central Nervous System (CNS), and AVAI therapeutic areas. What does this last one "AVAI" mean? AVAI means a classification of specialized therapeutic focus areas.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Medpace Holdings. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the conversion of expected revenue. The ending backlog for Q1 of 2026 was $2.9 billion. It is estimated that approximately $1.94 billion of backlog will be converted to revenue in the next 12 months. The risk here is that this is just a projection, and there is no assurance that most of this backlog will actually convert to revenue. In turn, this might mean that it could miss its higher revenue guidance for 2026. It is projecting revenues for full-year 2026 to be between $2.755B and $2.855B, which would end up being YoY increases of between 8.9% and 12.8%, respectively.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the "Net Book-to-Bill" number I mentioned above of 0.88x, which was achieved in Q1 of 2026. The reason why is because this was a steep drop from 1.04x in Q4 of 2025. This number is important because the Net Book-to-Bill is calculated by Net New Business Awards divided by revenue. Backlog cancellations reaching the highest in one year was the cause for this number to drop so drastically. There is a belief that this number could stabilize, but this remains a risk to watch regardless. If backlog cancellations start to accelerate at a rapid pace, then it would be very bad for this company going forward. What might mitigate such cancellations could be new pipeline products to be added and potential execution of its business model. However, this remains a major risk nonetheless.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of the direct costs from the company that had increased substantially. The reason why I state that is because direct costs for Q1 of 2026 were $510.3 million, compared to the prior year in the same quarter of only $380.2 million. Total direct costs include a multitude of items that could change in scope depending upon what is charged during the quarter. This ranges from labor, employee benefits, third-party service-related expenses, office rent, out-of-pocket expenses, and much more. The point here is that these direct costs are expensed as they are incurred. This means they are not at all deferred. The risk here is that such total direct costs could continue to increase, which could have a negative impact on the company's business.

Conclusion

As I mentioned above, Medpace Holdings had observed percentage growth in two key areas, which were in terms of revenues and "Net New Business Awards." With respect to revenue for Q1 of 2026, the reported number was $706.6 million, which was an increase of 26.5% from the prior year. The "Net New Business Awards" category had increased by 23.7% year-over-year. The company continues to execute on its business, as observed by these two metrics. Another positive is that the company has guided higher for its full-year 2026 earnings. It is projecting revenues for full-year 2026 to be between $2.755B and $2.855B for starters. It has also projected this year to have higher EBITDA and net income.

The reason for downgrading it to a "Buy" rating from a "Strong Buy" is because of the headwinds it faces. I think it can continue to execute as noted with the higher projected revenue guidance, but with caution because of the highest increase in backlog cancellations observed in a 1-year period. I remain optimistic, and for now I believe it can continue to deliver revenue growth as it has been. Now, if cancellations start to ramp up even further and result in a bigger drop in the "Net Book-to-Bill" numbers, then I will re-evaluate this stock again at such time. I might even downgrade it yet again to a "Hold" rating. With the beat of both the top and bottom lines of its earnings in Q1 of 2026, plus given higher full-year 2026 guidance, I believe for now I should downgrade this stock to a "Buy" rating.

data.title: CDAY:CA : Uncapped Canadian Exposure With S&P 500 Option Overlay
data.description:
Hamilton Enhanced Canadian Equity DayMAX™ ETF (CDAY:CA) was recently launched by Hamilton ETFs in July 2025. Hamilton Enhanced Canadian Equity DayMAX™ ETF (CDAY:CA) is a recent addition under Hamilton ETF’s series of funds. Launched on July 15, 2025, CDAY is part of a three-fund suite that Hamilton Capital Partners has marketed as Canada's first ETFs to use zero-days-to-expiry (0DTE) options. Although we have ample number of ETFs focused on producing incremental income in form of writing call options, primarily covered calls on their underlying holdings with usually predefined option writing levels.

In contrast, however, CDAY actually utilises a different strategy in substance other than what it looks, and it therefore differs from other option writing funds to a more pronounced extent than one might think at an initial glance. At present, the distribution yield with current semi-monthly distribution of CAD $0.1800 and a market price of $26.7 would come to ~16.2%.

CDAY primarily holds core exposure into Hamilton Champions Canadian Dividend Index ETF (CMVP:CA), another ETF which holds positions in well-known large-cap Canadian equities. This fund therefore provides investors with access to large-cap Canadian equities. Kindly also note that the underlying for call writing is neither CMVP:CA nor the Canadian equities. CMVP basically holds a stake in established Canadian companies distributing dividends with a history of stable and increasing dividends. CMVP attempts to track the performance of Solactive Canada Dividend Elite Champions Index to the extent possible. The current portfolio snapshot of holdings reflects this point.

The reason for discussing (CMVP:CA) is also that CDAY holds a position in CMVP:CA with ~100% portfolio exposure. Like I have already commented, CDAY does not write any options on Canadian equities. Basically, what this suggests is that virtually all of the total returns of CMVP:CA would eventually flow to the unitholders of CDAY. This is a very interesting aspect in this fund. To put it another way, an investor holding a position in CDAY would accrue all of the returns of CMVP:CA (before option overlays). There is an management expense overlay of ~0.85% which CDAY charges, but the fact is that if the remaining portfolio and strategy allocation, which is levered in nature, is able to provide incremental yield above the additional cost layer attributed to holding CMVP:CA via CDAY, then the proposition would make an economic sense.

At the same time, this also suggests that an investor, unless they are willing to have an exposure to Canadian equities as a primary focus area, might not want to allocate to this vehicle. Although the product is marketed as an 0DTE option strategy product, the key factor remains intact that the primary exposure is Canadian equities in form of tracking Solactive Canada Dividend Elite Champions Index, and only the secondary, levered portion of the fund is attributed to US equities and option writing overlay.

The performance results since the product launch also reflect this assertion. At the same time, performance evaluation to an extent might also suggest that there are in fact some merits in CDAY. However, the current history does not suggest any sufficient time range to warrant a reasonable conclusion for the rationale of small comparative outperformance and also the prospect of the same going forward. It is important to note that the primary benchmark, according to me, for analyzing CDAY remains the ETF it has a core indirect exposure to, which is CMVP:CA. If it is not able to achieve any incremental returns over the said ETF, then the leverage and option overlay portion of the fund would, in fact, be value deteriorating rather than enhancing.

With comparison to US equities, CMVP and as a result CDAY have performed fairly well over the recent period. In general, Canadian equities are resource and commodity heavy with a value-oriented tilt. This allows them to distribute a meaningful portion of their earnings as dividends and also to benefit from resource and commodity upcycles. Should the trend continue, Canadian equities might continue to outperform US equities. However, that remains cyclically driven rather than structural.

CDAY’s 0DTE Overlay — Structure and Implications

Before proceeding further to analyze if any investment proposition might be warranted for CDAY ETF, it is important to first understand in a little more depth the underlying option writing strategy possessed by CDAY. Like I mentioned in the beginning, the option overlay utilized by CDAY is in substance unique from other covered writing funds.

To understand that, let’s see how exactly CDAY structures its option portfolio book. Since CDAY doesn't publicly disclose either the results of its option writing book, however the prospectus allows the ETF to write options up to 100% of value of the position on either Canadian or U.S indexes.

The present aspects from the ETF however suggests that the fund typically writes options on the S&P 500 index to execute its 0DTE strategy. So, while the ETF is allowed to use discretion in call writing levels, what we might reasonably understand is at present it largely trends utilize S&P500 for that purpose. The above allocation in (VOO) which is an S&P 500 tracking ETF also reflects this aspect. We can have a reasonable inference that the fund allocates its secondary part of its exposure to the S&P 500 via Vanguard’s ETF, and likely executes 0DTE option strategies on that exposure. The reason for that is stated by the ETF itself that 0DTE expiration options are only currently only available select U.S indices including S&P 500. However, have a note that this is rather due to a practical utility and not the result of any mandate.

Since, the fund is allowed having ~25% leverage ratio which allows an overall portfolio exposure of 125%, the fund while having a 100% exposure to Canadian equities still allocates the remaining portion to U.S equities by VOO ETF and writes options on S&P500 indexes to carry out its daily options.

The other difference in 0DTE option writing is that, unlike other call writing strategies, this keeps the overnight upside intact. That means the reduction in upside, which is the structural issue in many covered call writing ETFs, is capped to at least a meaningful extent in the case of the present fund. However, note that 0DTE options do have their own set of issues such as fairly low premiums and very high gamma exposure (risk). To consider them as better than far-dated options is an incorrect assessment on the investor’s part, which largely happens due to falling for marketing narratives.

NAV Impact

Can 0DTE options allow the fund to sustain distributions without imparing NAVs? Given the fund's very short operating history, it would be imprudent to try to draw any suggestion on a conclusive manner from a single reporting period. Since inception to FY25 end, CDAY distributed $2.00 per unit during the period, of which $1.79, nearly 90%, was attributable as return of capital. Per-unit NAV nonetheless rose by $2.99 from operations, but $2.60 of that increase came from unrealized gains on the equity portfolio.

With reference to options, since these are written and settled the same day, option P&L would typically flow through the realized gains & losses Even if we were to attribute the entire $0.13 per unit of realized gains to the option overlay, the returns still would constitute option roughly 0.5% on beginning NAV over a ~6-month period.

The interpretation is simple, with current yield at around ~16%, the company would need to generate returns in investment appreciation to keep on funding distributions without impairing net asset values. It is fine even if it comes as a return of capital, with ETF having an unrealized appreciation in equities, which would also act as a tax benefits, but the ETF having, but when either the realized or unrealized gains would be inadequate to fund distributions, NAV would decline over that period. And option overlay should be considered only as incremental source of secondary alpha and not primarily, at least for the time being. On an overall basis, the portion is seemingly small at present to make a meaningful difference. However considering the short tenure of performance since initiation, it would be good if we wait for a quarter or two more to draw more reasonable assertion on option overlay.

In addition, the 0DTE overlay of ETF, which when also looked by present source of writing being S&P 500 which is again a secondary composition of the fund’s portfolio, the impact of the options overall remains moderate for the time being. Similarly, on the option outlook, the fund’s use of 0DTE options implies fairly low sensitivity to implied volatility levels compared to other covered call writing funds using long-tenure options, whose prices on a relative basis have far more impact from underlying market volatility and overnight uncertainty levels. The 0DTE exposure, while on the other hand comparatively carries far less Vega (the rate of sensitivity to volatility) and far higher gamma (the rate of change of delta), both of which aspects make the strategy far more contingent on portfolio manager execution rather than usual monthly covered call writings.

It would therefore, at least be good for the time being if investors pay more emphasis on the underlying Canadian exposure of the fund, while considering options only as a secondary incremental factor. The key performance driver remains Canadian equities.

A Case For an Allocation to CDAY

The holding proposition for CDAY would be viable only when an investor desires to allocate to established Canadian equities, which I highlighted earlier. The additional part, where the fund purchases US S&P 500 exposure via Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO) and writes options on the S&P 500, is the incremental aspect attributable to the fund. However, if that additional incremental factor is executed properly, it can generate a moderate but meaningful consistent alpha over the returns attributed by Canadian equities, while also providing, at least to an extent, a less correlated secondary source of performance attribution.

Coming to Canadian equities, we have already seen them performing better than broad-based US equities in recent time periods. When that happens, CDAY also realizes similar performance. Over the calendar year 2026, Canadian equities have seen better performance so far, which has been largely attributed to the energy uptrend and sharp energy price rise we saw year-to-date. This has to do with the value tilt and resource- and commodity-dominated economy of Canada, and as a result its listed equities. However, even at present, the valuation parameters of Canadian equities are seemingly better than those of broader US stock market equities such as the S&P 500. If we see the 10-year historical P/E ratios of US equities and Canadian equities, the present gap still is meaningfully wide.

Nevertheless, there might be other products and ETFs as well which might provide an investor with a similar exposure to Canadian equities, then why should an investor consider CDAY. The answer lies in distribution yields. Take the example of CMVP only, in which CDAY is primarily exposed. CMVP has a distribution yield of ~2.87%. On the contrary, CDAY has a current distribution yield of ~16.5%, considering the recent payouts of ~0.1880 with distributions paid semi-monthly versus once a month in the case of CMVP. This is particularly appealing to income-focused investors. Even though a portion of the distributions in CDAY's would be the result of NAV paybacks, which again do not imply any excess earnings yield or superior economics, still for certain investors focused on receiving regular distributions, the proposition still tends to appeal. The payback period, which is when an investor realizes all their money back in an investment, would tend to be significantly shorter in CDAY as compared to other Canadian pure-play long only equity funds. This is also to do with the fact that 0DTE option writing income, to an extent, supports the underlying distributions.

The fund saw an increase in price as well, which has contributed to total returns and is suggestive of the quality of underlying distributions. That has to do with strong recent performance of Canadian equities, and therefore even after paying distributions in excess of the income generated by the fund, CDAY was still able to increase its Net Asset Value. That would not have been the case if the underlying CMVP would not have seen an increase in its price around the same time. Therefore, I keep emphasizing the proposition is only worth it when one has a favorable opinion on Canadian equities, and the holding period is only till that favorable opinion is intact.

At present, therefore, considering relatively better valuations for Canadian equities with a naturally value-oriented tilt, as compared to the S&P 500, which is dominated by mega-cap tech stocks, would tend to make for a comparatively better allocation for income-seeking investors. The exposure to the former (Canadian equities) is in general based on present earning power of enterprises, while the latter is predominantly priced on growth prospects. A value-oriented investor, therefore, with an objective to seek distributions, would in general also tend to prefer the former.

In addition, the call overlay portion of the ETF, providing a chance to earn incremental returns over the returns attributed to the indirect holding companies of the fund, naturally tends to increase the appeal. This possibility of incremental benefit is provided to the investor for a ~0.85% additional management fee, in addition to ~0.19% charged by the underlying ETF, and seems to offer a fair cost-benefit proposition. If the incremental US portfolio addition and 0DTE option writing, in total, can over time provide the investor with an incremental ~2%–4% yield over the standalone exposure to those Canadian companies via CMVP, then the proposition is worthwhile. The execution of this incremental portion remains critical nevertheless.

The core proposition remains that of the attractiveness of Canadian equities, both with respect to higher dividend payouts and relatively lower multiples. In addition, at the current juncture, energy shocks resulting in elevation of Oil & Gas prices for geopolitical or other reasons would not only tend to benefit Canadian equities in general but also provide a useful hedge against energy-related market shocks to an investor. This is also reflected in sector composition between the indexes of Canadian securities and the S&P 500. As one might observe, Canadian equities are overweight in energy, financials, and materials, which pay higher dividends and are usually valued at current earnings power, versus the tech-heavy S&P 500 with a proposition based on capital appreciation due to future growth prospects. While there is nothing wrong with that, as highlighted earlier, the current price paid for those growth prospects, when compared to more value-tilted equities such as Canada, is quite high, making them relatively less attractive versus value-oriented securities.

CDAY is Rated a Buy at Present

Considering all of the aspects, the nature of primary holding of CDAY being Canadian equities, the secondary allocation to US equities for executing covered call writing using ultra-short same-day expiry (0DTE) options, and a favourable outlook for Canadian equities in terms of seeking dividend distributions versus growth-dominated broad-based equities, seems to offer value-for-money for investors with a constructive view on Canadian equities. In addition, the regular higher distribution yield of CDAY naturally increases the appeal of the fund for an income-oriented investor. Further, the underlying index which CMVP attempts to replicate has outperformed the Canadian benchmark S&P/TSX 60 over a long period of time, a fact which the ETF prominently highlights. This also suggests that there are indeed merits to investing in Canadian equities via CMVP.

Limitations to Investment Proposition

There are additional aspects, apart from risk factors inherent in every investment, which an investor might want to consider. Foremost, there is no investment merit whatsoever in the current fund without a favourable view on Canadian equities. There is neither any point in evaluating the option component without a simultaneous favourable opinion on Canadian equities.

Secondly, 0DTE options possess many risks, such as black swan events which might cause significant losses to the option portfolio. Similarly, a single trade gone wrong due to any factor has the potential to wipe out gains and profits of months and even years of small premiums written. 0DTE is inherently dependent on VIX and volatility levels, and an eventual trade-off exists for earning incremental yield. The tail risk concentration in a single expiry, with a very high exposure to gamma, has its own consequences which need active monitoring by portfolio managers. The strategy in general would work in stable to moderately volatile markets. Therefore, it is environment-dependent and exposed to many contingent factors outside the manager’s control.

In the current case, an investor, by allocating around ~25% to US equities, tends to risk underperformance relative to Canadian equities if drawdowns in US exposure are larger than what the fund can generate through writing options on those holdings. Eventually, this incremental allocation is not a risk-free proposition based on any pricing arbitrage; it is an additional exposure taken on with the possibility of generating incremental yield, and the investor needs to be aware of that. Also, like in the case of other high-distribution funds, in case of drawdowns or low-market price appreciation, distributions would come via NAV reduction, leaving the fund with less capital to compound, a point which an investor again needs to be aware of. Higher distribution yields contain trade-offs as well. Therefore, the proposition remains more objective-oriented rather than structural.

In Conclusion

Canadian equities tend to trade at relatively better valuations as compared to broad-based US equities at present. In addition, the new launch by Hamilton to use 0DTE options to execute a covered call strategy seems to be of interest. Paying ~0.85%, considering the active nature of the strategy and the fact that trades ought to be executed almost every day, does not seem excessive either. Therefore, CDAY could be considered by an investor desiring to receive continuous distributions without being very much concerned about whether these are attributable to price appreciation or NAV paybacks (NAV of CDAY would have appreciated more, if it had provided lower distributions) and with a favorable view on Canadian equities. The holding period for an investor for this ETF is identical to the time that favorable outlook on Canadian equities remains, and therefore this thesis might require frequent monitoring and should not be viewed as a structural income-generating product. That caution should be kept in mind while allocating.

data.title: Intel: The Prodigal Son Returns (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
In early February, I downgraded Intel Corporation (INTC) from a Hold to a Sell rating. I stated that the stock was facing a reckoning as I found signs that the company's turnaround was, in fact, losing momentum. Furthermore, the sharp expansion in the valuation showed that a lot of optimism was getting priced in.

As you probably know by now, Intel reported their latest batch of earnings yesterday (April 23rd), and the stock rocketed in after-hours trading. The rating history chart below actually hasn't updated to reflect the extent of these gains, as Intel is now around 65% above levels when my previous update was published. Today, I will conduct a fresh analysis to see whether my pessimism was misplaced or whether the market is getting increasingly irrational.

Below, it is shown that a turnaround is finally starting to gain traction. Revenue growth has bounced back into positive territory, all segments are seeing considerable improvement, and their margins are expanding significantly. Looking forward, guidance is impressive and shows that the recovery will continue. The prodigal son seems to be finally returning, but that may not necessarily mean the stock is a buy now. Currently, the biggest risk to stock outperformance would be the highly elevated valuation. With a successful turnaround already priced in, I would say that Intel has become only a hold at this point.

Q1 Results Overview

Let's start with an overview of Intel's Q1 results before moving into a deeper dive into each of their segments. Firstly, for the top line, the chipmaker generated total revenues of $13.6 billion, up 7% YoY. While this isn't the strongest growth rate in the world, it does represent a significant improvement from the 4% decline that was seen just last quarter. This may show that an inflection point is in and that business activity is finally starting to rebound in a material manner.

Another encouraging sign was the fact that they blasted past analysts top-line expectations by a stunning margin of $1.15 billion. Given where their quarterly revenues stand right now, this is an indication of major outperformance, and there may now be signs that their recovery is proceeding quicker than many had expected.

Looking beyond their top line, there are promising signs in their margins as well. For gross margin, they saw 1.8 percentage points of expansion. This is a stark contrast to the 4.2 percentage points of deterioration seen in their Q4, and so it seems that they are tightening cost controls in an effective manner.

Strength continued on into their adjusted operating margin as well. This key profitability metric also saw a large swing from the previous quarter, as there were 0.8 percentage points of deterioration in Q4 versus 6.9 percentage points of expansion in Q1. This sizeable YoY improvement dwarfs the gross margin expansion, and that clearly shows the company is successfully streamlining their operations and corporate activities to a great degree. Apart from a top-line growth inflection point, margin improvement is a trademark sign of a turnaround, and so Intel has impressed me.

With revenue growth back into positive territory and margins improving considerably, the bottom line saw strength that seemingly shocked Wall Street. For Q1, they reported an adjusted EPS of $0.29, up 123% YoY. This is, of course, a robust growth rate. What's even more noteworthy is the fact that they destroyed analyst expectations by $0.28. Consensus estimates expected just $0.01 for EPS, and so Intel is storming back in a manner that caught plenty of investors off guard.

Client Computing Group

While the 1% revenue growth rate for the CCG segment may not be the most exciting, I would say that it is a signal of resilience in the company's traditional business. Just in the previous quarter, there was a 3% decline in revenues. With memory cost headwinds having notable effects on the PC market, the ability for Intel to steady the ship for this segment isn't an easy feat in my view. In their earnings, the company cited healthy demand for their Series 3, and it is reportedly their "best product launch in five years." With CCG still being their largest segment by revenue, it is nice to see that things are stabilizing.

Data Center and AI

With the AI boom in full swing, there is no question that all eyes are on their DCAI segment, and results in Q1 definitely didn't disappoint. This business generated $5.1 billion worth of revenues, up 22% YoY. There was just growth of 5% in the previous quarter, and so it is clear that this key business is now gaining significant momentum. They reportedly entered into long-term supply agreements, and their ASICs revenues nearly doubled YoY.

Earlier this month, Intel and Google (GOOGL) significantly deepened their relationship. Google Cloud continues to use Xeon processors to power tasks including AI and inference, and the companies are also working together to develop ASIC-based infrastructure processing units (IPUs). Therefore, it is clear that Intel is playing an increasingly large role in the AI market.

Foundry

While Foundry is still a drag on the company's profitability at this point, there is no question that this business is becoming increasingly important as the United States focuses on onshoring the chip manufacturing process. In Q1, revenues saw 16% growth, which is a significant acceleration from Q4's 3% growth rate. They also stated that Intel 18A and 14A progress is ahead of expectations.

Just very recently, Elon Musk stated that Tesla (TSLA) and SpaceX (SPACE) will use Intel's 14A manufacturing process for the Terafab project. This is a major vote of confidence by an industry titan, and so it seems that their Foundry business is making inroads as well.

Guidance Above Expectations

Intel provided Q2 guidance that was above expectations for the top and bottom line. For revenue, the midpoint of the above range would imply an 11% YoY growth rate. That would be an acceleration of growth from what we saw in Q1, and so this helps to confirm that an inflection point is indeed in and that business activity will continue to strengthen moving forward.

For gross margin, they expect 9.3 percentage points of improvement, which is much larger than the expansion that we saw in Q1. As stated earlier, margin expansion is one of the most important signs of a turnaround.

Lastly, the $0.20 EPS projection is a major change in fortunes from 2025 Q2's $0.10 loss per share, and so this is another sign that profitability is headed in the right direction. As a whole, I view Q2 guidance as firmly robust and indicative of a strengthening turnaround effort from the company.

Valuation Is The Biggest Risk

While the turnaround is finally materializing in a strong manner now, the valuation is probably the largest risk to stock outperformance moving forward. It was reported today that Intel stock eclipsed its dot-com era peak and that their forward P/E is now the highest of any large-cap chip stock. These may be signs that things are getting a little frothy, and so the risk/reward may not be as attractive as many believe.

As you can see in the table above, my preferred measure, the non-GAAP forward P/E, is at an elevated reading of 120.24. This multiple is currently at a 413.65% premium to the IT sector. Another key measure is the non-GAAP PEG ratio, and there is quite a sizeable 58.3% premium there as well. At this point, it seems that the stock is pricing in a very successful turnaround already, and for investors, that means opportunity may now be limited.

At this point, Intel's turnaround has gained significant steam. Revenue growth has hit an inflection point, all segments are seeing an improvement in business trajectory, and strong margin expansion suggests effective cost and expense control measures. Guidance was also impressive, and so I would say this is as strong as the recovery has ever looked.

The problem is that forward earnings projections would need to grow more than fivefold to bring the forward P/E back to the sector's median. In my view, this gives Intel relatively little room for error, and so the risk/reward is only neutral at best right now, even as the outlook brightens.

Conclusion

The prodigal son has returned with a boom. In Q1, there were plenty of signs that the fundamentals were rebounding for the chipmaker. The top and bottom line beats show that this caught many investors off guard. In my previous article, the turnaround showed signs of losing momentum, and so it has been a remarkable shift in such a short period of time. Moving forward, all signs point towards continued recovery, but the valuation is a major obstacle to further stock outperformance from here. With the stock's price tag being where it is, an entry now would make little sense in my view. Therefore, I have decided to upgrade Intel stock, but to just a Hold rating at this juncture.

data.title: Apple Bulls Show Weakness
data.description:
By Anton Kharitonov

Apple (AAPL) is still trading in an event-driven mode, with the market simultaneously digesting the CEO transition, AI expectations, and the upcoming April 30 earnings report. The news that Tim Cook is preparing to hand control to John Ternus is being viewed not as a crisis but as a managed transition, although the leadership change still adds an uncertainty premium to the stock.

The key question for Apple’s valuation today is no longer just the iPhone cycle, but whether the company can plug into the AI trend without losing its premium multiple. Right now, Apple still looks more like a follower than a leader: Microsoft (MSFT) already has its OpenAI link, Google (GOOG, GOOGL) has Gemini, and Apple is reportedly leaning on a Google partnership for future Siri AI features. That is why the market is not giving Apple the same AI premium as NVDA or MSFT, and the stock is still treated as a high-quality but not yet dominant AI beneficiary.

Ahead of earnings, the market will focus on three things: iPhone sales, Services growth, and guidance. iPhone remains the backbone of the story, but Services matters more for margin quality and re-rating potential because it shows how much value the ecosystem can generate beyond hardware. If Apple delivers a strong outlook, the market may read it as evidence that the company can maintain growth even through leadership change and an unfinished AI narrative.

Structurally, the stock still looks range-bound rather than impulsive. Investors are not yet willing to price in a strong AI breakout, but they are also not fully pricing in a negative succession scenario, so the tape is mostly waiting for a catalyst. The bullish case still depends on ecosystem resilience, strong cash flow, and evidence that AI will become an additional driver rather than a threat to the business model.

So Apple is trading with a mildly positive bias, but the bulls are losing momentum. At this stage, they do not have enough strength to break resistance around $275, which could trigger profit-taking and a decline toward $268-265. A move above that resistance before earnings looks unlikely, but if it does happen, the next upside zone would be $278-280.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: KKR Real Estate: A Dividend Cut And Book Value Dip Heightens Preferreds' Role
data.description:
KKR Real Estate Finance Trust Inc. (KREF) has cut its common share dividend as it continues to transition its commercial real estate ("CRE") loan portfolio away from legacy office property loans and institutes a $75 million stock repurchase program. KREF is set to declare a quarterly cash dividend of $0.10 per share, a material 60% reduction from its prior distribution, and $0.40 per share annualized for a 6.56% dividend yield. The mREIT's dividend payment history has unfortunately been defined by consecutive cuts and poor coverage. There have been two cuts over the last 5 years, with the quarterly distribution down from $0.43 per share in 2021.

The dividend is down 76.7% over the last half a decade, with KREF's common shares down 70% over the same time frame. This has always placed KREF's 6.50% Series A Preferred Shares (KREF.PR.A) as the more prudent investment choice. I last covered KREF as a Buy, with the mREIT down since then on the back of the dividend cut and widening credit spreads as higher downstream inflation expectations spark risk-off sentiment and a selloff in U.S. Treasuries.

KREF reported a first-quarter total interest income of $95.91 million, down from $113.97 million in the year-ago comp on the back of a total senior CRE loan portfolio that was down 16.4% year-over-year to $5.1 billion from $6.1 billion. The mREIT had a weighted average unlevered all-in yield of 7.2% as of the end of the first quarter, down around 40 basis points from 7.6% in the year-ago period. Hence, KREF is seeing both yield compression and a nominal dip in the broad size of its senior CRE loan portfolio. This has driven the weakness in interest income, with net interest income for the first quarter coming in at $26.19 million, down from $31.34 million in the year-ago period.

Critically, the mREIT reported a first-quarter book value of $11.87 per share, a material 17.80% decline from $14.44 per share in the year-ago comp. KREF is trading hands at a 48.70% discount to book value per share as of the end of the first quarter. This discount has partially de-risked a long position in the commons but has proved to be more of a liability than an investment opportunity. This has meant seeking the safety of the preferreds. These pay out a cumulative and perpetual $1.625 per share annual coupon for a 9.12% current yield. This means you can earn 256 basis points more in income over the commons, sit higher on KREF's capital stack, and materially reduce the risk of losing parts of your income. The preferreds function as a type of hybrid fixed-income security, with 13,110,000 shares of the Series A issued and outstanding as of the end of the first quarter. This means it costs KREF $21.30 million per year to service this series of preferreds, 15.72% of its cash and cash equivalents of $135.44 million as of the end of the first quarter.

Originations, Credit Quality, And Interest Income Dip

The mREIT originated a floating-rate senior loan of $184 million during the first quarter, with $415 million in loan repayments, inclusive of $312 million of repayments on office loans. KREF's exposure to office loans now represents 18% of its total portfolio, with the mREIT aiming to reduce these legacy loans to under 10% by the end of 2026. Multifamily remains its largest allocation at 41% of the portfolio, with the second largest allocation coming from industrial properties, which formed 22% of the total CRE loan portfolio. These formed the backdrop for a first-quarter net loss of $0.96 per share, with KREF's CECL provision forming $1.14 per share of this. The mREIT's weighted average risk rating came in at 3.3, a partial deterioration from a weighted average risk rating of 3.1 in the year-ago quarter.

KREF had loans worth $704 million on its first-quarter watch list, with the mREIT downgrading four loans during the quarter while upgrading a previously risk-rated 5 Cambridge life science loan to a 3 rating following a modification and restructure. The most salient risk factor facing the preferreds will come from inflation expectations and U.S. Treasury rates. The U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) is up around 35 basis points from its low in February following the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, with energy prices spiking on the back of this, and prior expectations that the Fed will cut rates by at least 50 basis points this year now reduced to zero, according to the CME FedWatch Tool. KREF's distributable earnings were negative at $0.06 per share, with around $0.27 of this from a realized loss.

Conclusion

KREF's dividend cut is substantial, and the continued erosion of book value means it's hard to recommend the common shares as a Buy. I continue to own the preferreds. I am rating KREF as a Hold.

data.title: U.S. Consumer Sentiment Under Pressure From War And Rising Oil Prices
data.description:
By Krzysztof Kamiński

U.S. consumer sentiment deteriorated sharply in April, falling to a record low. The University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index declined to 49.8 points from 53.3 points in March, marking the weakest reading in data going back to 1978. The decline was driven mainly by concerns over the economic consequences of the war with Iran, rising energy prices, and persistent uncertainty about the geopolitical outlook.

The deterioration was visible in both consumers’ assessment of current conditions and their expectations for the months ahead. The current conditions index fell to its lowest level in four months, while the expectations index dropped to its weakest level in almost a year. Particularly concerning is the assessment of households’ future financial situation, which reached its lowest level since May of the previous year. The data suggest that U.S. consumers are increasingly feeling the impact of higher energy costs and the risk of weaker economic conditions.

Inflation expectations are rising again

The most important warning signal is the sharp increase in inflation expectations. Consumers now expect prices to rise by 4.7% over the next year, compared with 3.8% in March. Long-term inflation expectations, covering the next five to ten years, rose to 3.5%, the highest level since October. This suggests that fears of persistent price pressure are intensifying again, even though markets had previously expected inflation to gradually ease.

Fuel prices are the main source of concern. Around two-thirds of respondents expect gasoline prices to be higher in a year, with the average expected increase at nearly 50 cents per gallon. For households, this means a risk of higher everyday costs, while for the broader economy it raises the possibility of weaker consumer spending in the coming months. Although retail sales remain relatively solid, a further increase in energy prices could limit Americans’ willingness to spend.

The Strait of Hormuz is disrupting the oil market

Pressure on fuel prices is directly linked to the tense situation in the oil market. According to Goldman Sachs, oil production in Gulf countries is currently 14.5 million barrels per day lower than before the war, representing a 57% decline in regional output. The main problem remains the near-total halt in traffic through the Strait of Hormuz, one of the world’s most important oil transport routes.

The disruption of this strategic passage is significantly reducing supply and keeping tension elevated in the global energy market. Goldman Sachs analysts estimate that even a full and safe reopening of the strait, combined with no further attacks, would not lead to an immediate return of production to previous levels. Restoring output could take several months, and the longer the disruption continues, the slower the normalization process may become.

Brent crude trades near USD 105 per barrel

Brent crude prices are rising for a fifth consecutive day and are heading for a weekly gain of around 17%. On Friday, Brent was trading near USD 105 per barrel, compared with less than USD 73 before the outbreak of the war. Although Brent remains below the conflict-period peak of nearly USD 120 per barrel, current levels still represent strong cost pressure for both consumers and businesses.

Such a sharp rise in oil prices increases the risk of renewed inflation acceleration, especially if higher energy costs begin to feed through into transport, production, and consumer goods prices. For U.S. households, this means greater financial uncertainty, while for the Federal Reserve, it creates a more difficult policy environment. Rising inflation expectations and weaker consumer sentiment may complicate the assessment of whether the economy needs support or whether monetary policy should remain cautious.

Risks to consumer spending are increasing

The University of Michigan data show that U.S. consumers are reacting more strongly to the consequences of the conflict with Iran and rising energy prices. The temporary ceasefire between the U.S. and Iran reduces the risk of further escalation, but the lack of a lasting agreement means uncertainty remains high. As long as the situation around the Strait of Hormuz remains unstable, oil prices may stay elevated and inflation concerns may continue to weigh on household sentiment.

As a result, the U.S. economy is facing an increasingly complex challenge. On the one hand, consumption has not collapsed. On the other hand, record-low sentiment, rising inflation expectations, and more expensive fuel increase the risk of weaker spending in the coming months. If energy prices remain high, pressure on household budgets could become one of the key factors limiting U.S. economic growth.

Original Post

data.title: Adobe: My Industry Experience Vs. Market Panic, Why This Is A Strong Buy
data.description:
Adobe Inc. (ADBE) stock has fallen nearly 40% since my last report published nearly a year ago. In my view, generative AI was a risk to the business, but adopting AI also provides Adobe with opportunities to provide AI tools to its customers. That, however, could not prevent the stock price from collapsing. In this report, I review my valuation and rating for Adobe, as I believe the stock may no longer recapture its premium valuation, but the stock price decline triggers an upgrade to a Strong Buy rating.

My Experience With Adobe

When I write about Adobe, it is not just another company I am writing about. Prior to becoming an analyst on Seeking Alpha and with some overlap, I worked for an award-winning company that leveraged Adobe Photoshop and Illustrator to generate high-definition textures for flight simulator products. After that, I worked for a creative agency that leveraged the full Adobe product suite to generate corporate marketing material and commercials. So, I have years of experience in Adobe, and looking at where AI was a year ago, I could not imagine that it would be preferred by professional agencies over Adobe.

In some way, that may be part of the problem. Adobe uses a subscription-heavy model, which is not necessarily the best choice for consumers, and those consumers may elect to use free or freemium generative AI tools, and from there a spillover could occur to professional use cases. Compared to a year ago, generative AI has taken a leap from the point where it was mostly unusable from a creative perspective to becoming a fine way to quickly generate material. Adobe has also integrated AI across its ecosystem with Firefly AI for image generation, AI assistants, and AI integration for automation and marketing.

However, even as a big proponent and user of Adobe tools, I can only say that the company did not use its edge in creative design tools to generate a similar edge over competing generative AI tools. The company’s AI selling point is commercial-safe AI, which means it can be used in marketing material and for sales, but that seemingly is not good enough. Furthermore, we note that Adobe’s Firefly model at times simply refuses to generate, and integrations with ChatGPT often are not working at all. So, whereas Adobe had a big opportunity to capitalize on generative AI, they seem to be highly restrictive, and whatever image I could get out of Firefly wasn’t necessarily impressive in the sense that I could detect no quality edge over easier-to-use generative AI tools.

So, Adobe does own the creative pipeline, but it doesn’t really live up to expectations of creative freedom with AI or quality.

Adobe Earnings Are Far From Awful

The fiscal Q1 results show that the company booked 12% growth in revenues to $6.4 billion, including 13% subscription growth. Gross profit increased 12.6%, pointing at a 50 basis point expansion in the margins. Operating expenses grew 13.2%, driven by higher marketing costs driven by higher loss contingencies. Operating income grew 11.8% to $2.4 billion, indicating margins contracting slightly from 37.9% to 37.8%. So, it is not the case that Adobe is seeing growth evaporate or even decline. The market is pricing the same growth and strong asset and extension library that lead to customer retention at a lower multiple.

Adobe Stock Entry Point Looks More Compelling

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

While I can definitely agree that Adobe is no longer worth a premium as big as it was in prior years, we see that the stock price is currently well below that of a peer group valuation and also below the 3-year EV/EBITDA for Adobe. At roughly 8x EV/EBITDA, Adobe’s position in the market, in my view, is understated. Sales are expected to grow 9.3% annually, and a 6.5% CAGR on EBITDA does point at substantial margin decline with only 5% free cash flow growth. However, even with those less favorable growth rates, we note that the stock is undervalued against a peer group valuation.

My price target is based on a peer group valuation and current-year expected earnings of $367.90, and that would already indicate over 30% upside without assigning any premium to Adobe compared to peers.

We quite clearly see that the margins are set to come down drastically, but free cash flow generation remains more than healthy, with the company expected to be in a net cash position even when buying back $10 billion worth of shares annually. In that regard, I do believe that the valuation reset for Adobe is far too aggressive, valuing it as a subpar company while it is still the standard for digital media and creative agencies.

Conclusion: Adobe Is Now A Strong Buy

Adobe’s growth is expected to flatten somewhat. However, what we are currently seeing is that the company is priced as a subpar software company while the Adobe suite is still the go-to suite for creative agencies. Furthermore, there are high switching costs, and Adobe has an asset and extension library that generative AI does not match. I do believe that in terms of generative AI, the company is not living up to what it could have been, but that doesn’t justify the current valuation one bit. As a result, I am upgrading the stock to Strong Buy.

data.title: Why Markets Have Been Climbing Despite Wall Of Worry
data.description:
Equities are hovering near highs despite persistent inflation concerns, resilient economic data, and ongoing geopolitical tension. That’s raising questions about whether investors are appropriately pricing risk. Damian Fernandes, Managing Director & Co-Head of Global Equity Portfolio Management at TD Asset Management, weighs in on market resilience, risk, and what it means for positioning.

Transcript

Kim Parlee: Markets have been navigating a complex backdrop, resilient economic data, persistent inflation questions, and a steady drumbeat of geopolitical risk. Despite that, equities continue to hold near highs, raising questions about how much uncertainty is truly being priced in. For perspective, we're joined by Damian Fernandes of TD Asset Management.

It is always lovely to have you here.

Damian Fernandes: Always a pleasure.

Kim Parlee: Let's start with what I just said. At a high level, how would you characterize US markets right now? Because again, they seem stubbornly high. Which, I'm not complaining. I'm just saying, they seem stubbornly high.

Damian Fernandes: Yeah. I think if we actually rewind the clock and it was February 28 and the war was just about to start, and we are six weeks from that, six or seven weeks from that, and we're at breaking to new all-time highs yesterday, you would be surprised. And I think what's actually happened is that people have overestimated geopolitical risk and underestimated economic and earnings resiliency. Let me just be a little bit more--

Kim Parlee: Sorry, say it again. Do you think they're overestimating geopolitical risk?

Damian Fernandes: I think they are overestimating geopolitical risk, because markets priced at the margin. Think about our more recent geopolitical events, right? We can go back to COVID. The market bottomed before-- cases continued to rise. The market understood that COVID was significant--

Kim Parlee: But it was going to end.

Damian Fernandes: They saw a path-- the market bottomed up, I think, a long time before-- and cases continued to rise that whole time. Or, tariffs last year. I think tariffs-- the market's actually using the tariff playbook before we even had an agreement or how tariffs would be implemented. And as soon as the President of the United States said that we're open to negotiations, the market moved beyond that. So, we didn't even have the framework for what the rates were. The market had bottomed.

And so I think markets are pricing at the margin. Right now, the playbook is, similar to tariffs, is that, OK, we're open to negotiation. It's like, OK, so we know that the Strait, while detrimental, remains closed. And if it stays closed, can actually have significant economic ramifications. The fact that we can see a path to an opening at some point, I think that's what's being priced in. So, overestimating.

And the flip side of that is underestimating economic resiliency or earnings growth. I think last year, the US did 5.5%-- nominal GDP. We're on track to do that this year. We're going to do double-digits earnings growth. That's the backdrop. And I think what's really interesting-- and we can talk about this a little later-- is that even during this tumultuous March month, earnings revisions, estimates from analysts, and what they think companies are going to deliver, they kept being pushed higher.

So I think the backdrop-- look, I'm not panglossian, the world is great. I do think there's risks. But if at the margin those risks are more likely to ameliorate as opposed to accelerate or get worse, I think the market is moving beyond that. I am surprised, though, to your point, that we're punching through all-time highs. But look at the-- is it something that it's like, oh, I think I'm shocked? No.

Kim Parlee: Yeah.

Damian Fernandes: I think it's behaving somewhat rationally.

Kim Parlee: Can I ask, though, are there-- we talk about the "market," and there is no market.

Damian Fernandes: Sure.

Kim Parlee: There's, of course, many markets. Do you see any markets right now that are at greater risk or where you see greater opportunity? I mean, when you start to separate it out.

Damian Fernandes: Well, where we are right now in North America, we're relatively-- while there is pain at the gas pump for higher prices, and that affects consumption spend, broadly, North America is a net energy exporter. So higher energy prices actually are really good for Canada in terms of improving our terms of trade. But the US is a net exporter of energy too. The US, actually the Fed-- the deficit is helped by higher energy prices.

Kim Parlee: Even though individual consumers--

Damian Fernandes: Exactly. But the rest of the world, particularly countries in Europe, Asia are importers of energy prices. In Europe, they have fixed budgets. The EU, depending on the-- isn't allowed to run deficits. So, if a bigger proportion of your government expenditure is spent on procuring higher energy, that means you have less money for social services, for stimulus, for defense spending-- all the stuff that was supposed to promote economic growth. So the way to think about this, Kim, is that the longer the Strait stays closed, the more impactful it will be for countries that are net importers of energy, where you'll have to take down growth numbers in those. And you're already seeing that, by the way.

Kim Parlee: And I guess all the geopolitical implications that come from that as well. Not market-related, but the longer things stay closed, the longer people then start to do more of that multipolar, keep your own stuff, make--

Damian Fernandes: Keep your own stuff, there's hoarding.

Kim Parlee: Yes.

Damian Fernandes: People will hoard commodities. Because they will think that the longer it stays closed, I do not want to face this again, similar to what happened post-COVID-- just, people hoard. And so, that will provide-- I don't think commodity prices are going to get back to where before this war started. I just think that you'll see higher commodity prices because there'll be a geopolitical risk premium built in.

Kim Parlee: Let me take a look at a chart that you brought in. And this one, very catchy title. Number of rate cuts expected by December 2026. I kid. Tell me why you think this is important to look at.

Damian Fernandes: Yeah, I think it's really interesting. Because at the start of the year, if we go back to December 31, before this year started, a lot of the enthusiasm in the markets was actually being built on the likelihood that we'll see accommodation-- said plainly, central banks will ease. Inflation will fall, central banks will ease. What's actually happened is that because the war has increased inflationary impulse-- particularly like, we talked about gas prices-- central bankers around the world are now on the back foot.

I can understand it. Because if you remember, 2021, they left rates at zero when inflation was rising. It's almost like they are-- it's like muscle memory. They don't want to be charged with doing something like that again. So, they're probably more likely to maybe not raise rates, but not cut. And so what you're seeing in that chart is that we started the year with the market expecting 2 and 1/2 rate cuts for the full year. As we are today, we're talking about not even one rate cut. So for us, in equity markets or just even in fixed income, it means the anticipation of easing monetary policy, of lower rates to help stimulate the economy, that's-- the longer this stays and the more there's inflationary impulses, it's less likely to happen.

Kim Parlee: I don't want to dwell on this, but in terms of the politicization of the Fed, how does that impact this chart in terms of doing what the data tells you versus doing what your "boss" tells you?

Damian Fernandes: Yeah. So obviously, we have the nominee for--

Kim Parlee: Yes.

Damian Fernandes: --who's selected today, and I listened to a few of the things. And my simple thing is, what does he go to say in-- he was going to be-- as he was going to toe the line and be as pragmatic and judicious and say-- but I think longer term, I do think that Fed independence, in the opening remarks by Warsh, he did mention that that's paramount.
