And so, as much as people as Donald Trump selected him-- and Donald Trump wants lower rates-- these are-- He was at the Fed, he understands the importance or the gravitas of actually central bank independent policymaking. So, do I think he's going to cut rates immediately? No. And I think he probably has an excuse now, because inflation has been ticking up. So he's like, hey, you selected me to cut rates, but the facts have changed. So I do think this actually perversely works in his favor.

Kim Parlee: Damian, you talked about healthy GDP growth. We were just talking earlier about-- I think you said 5-6% growth. And then--

Damian Fernandes: Nominal GDP, yep.

Kim Parlee: Nominal GDP, thank you. That matters, especially now. But you've also got a great chart here I want to bring up, talking about the, to quote you, "learnings from the earnings." Again, why are we looking at this? Except we see that huge chart on the left going up to the right. Is that why?

Damian Fernandes: Yeah. I think that's-- so, all the other lines are averages of earnings historically. You normally start the year with really enthusiastic earnings estimates. As companies report, the numbers taper down. This year has been unique is that you started the year at a really strong level, we're talking about double-digit growth. But even as this war is transpiring and analysts are revising the numbers, instead of taking numbers down-- and they have lots of chances to do this-- they've actually upgraded the numbers. They're coming from things like tech, or they're coming from energy. But the rest of the market, too, hasn't seen a significant level of downgrades.

So, it's my point earlier-- I think we're underestimating the resiliency of economic activity and earnings growth. Earnings, uniquely, have been revised up, even though it's a pretty tumultuous time. And I think that's important.

Kim Parlee: And you said the earnings are being revised up on already what are being reported some very strong earnings.

Damian Fernandes: Exactly.

Kim Parlee: And I know we were chatting in the break about JPMorgan (JPM), Goldman Sachs (GS), maybe some others. What are you seeing?

Damian Fernandes: So last week, we just started we just started earnings season, and these next two weeks will be really, really critical, both for the outlook and because companies will have a chance to maybe comment on it. But so far, if we mark to market what happened last week, the numbers that-- I'll just use JPMorgan, the biggest bank in the US. Revenues at JPMorgan were up close to 10%. Earnings were up 17%. Return on common equity was at 23%. The numbers that Goldman and Morgan Stanley-- revenues were up double digits. Earnings were up 25% and 30%.

Kim Parlee: Why? Why are they so strong?

Damian Fernandes: It's a mix of-- so it's a mix of, obviously, the trading activity in the last month helped. So, like fixed-income, commodities. So trading activity helped.

But actually, the underlying business, there's not a significant degree of loan losses coming through. Spending, consumer spending continues to be fine, credit card spending. So, it's a host of things that's-- and the yield curve is positively sloped. They make money when you have a positively sloped yield curve.

So, the banks are actually telling you that the consumer and corporate America is fine. And then the other companies that reported last week, I thought was really interesting. We had Delta (DAL) report. And Delta reported a high single, I think close to 10% revenue growth, and I think close to 30% EPS growth. But on the call, the management team said that they're not really seeing a pullback in demand. In fact, bookings for March were fine, and they've continued into Q2. So, the consumer, at least on this continent, is continuing to spend.

Kim Parlee: Yeah. Which is interesting, because you get the headlines, people saying high gas prices impacting people. So this is about bifurcation.

Damian Fernandes: Completely. But the headlines of high gas prices are being offset by the headlines no one talks about, by fatter tax refunds. And we've talked about this numerous times before-- the narrative is around negativity. Let's just talk about high gas prices, but let's not talk about that tax refunds are also significantly--

Kim Parlee: If it leads, it leads. This is what they--

Damian Fernandes: Exactly. And look, I think we're in an understated economic revival. You have tax policy incentivizing a huge CapEx boom. You're seeing it in the earnings numbers. But I guess the fact that it isn't widely discussed allows opportunity for investors.

Kim Parlee: I've got a minute. I'm trying to think what I want to ask you with my one last minute, because I got a lot of things. I guess maybe I'll ask, for someone who's watching, and again, everyone has to do--

Damian Fernandes: Sure.

Kim Parlee: --their own research and figure out their own plans, what works for them. But how should people be thinking about asset allocation?

Damian Fernandes: Yeah, it's probably one of my favorite things. The idea of no free lunch. Our CIOs talked about this before. Going into this conflict, you didn't think you needed energy stocks, or you didn't think you needed Canadian investments and stuff, or people were selling all their US-- it really paid to have a diversified portfolio. And I think that continues to be one of the ways that-- look, I have no-- to be honest, Kim, I have no idea how the world is going to transpire six or eight months from now, but I do know a diversified portfolio will actually provide ballast, both against the opportunities and against-- and I think people have to continue to focus on that.

Kim Parlee: Alright. I do have time for one last question. But the last time we talked, I think you talked about the Mag 7 as the Lag 7. Still true?

Damian Fernandes: Well, I think the Mag 7-- that's the one thing from this earnings-- learnings from earnings-- I'll actually be watching, is how much they're promising to spend on their capital expenditure. Because so much of the economy, of the growth, of the earnings that you're seeing is driven by these Mag 7 companies spending stupendous amounts on capital expenditure. We can debate whether it's going to be the learn and return on investment. But the here and now, the numbers were 700 billion for this year on data center CapEx for AI. I want to see if that number is being revised up or down. I think that'll be very important at the margin for the market.
